items.prompt_md: What does the expression `i & (i - 1)` do to the binary representation of `i`?
options: Clears (zeroes out) the lowest set (1) bit
Reverses all the bits of i
items.answer: Clears (zeroes out) the lowest set (1) bit
items.explanation_md: `i - 1` flips all bits below and including i's lowest set bit; AND-ing with `i` keeps only the bits that survive that flip, which zeroes exactly the lowest set bit and leaves everything above it unchanged.

items.prompt_md: You need the number of 1-bits for every integer from 0 to n, and counting each number's bits independently costs O(n log n) overall. Which approach fits?
options: Precompute with a bitwise DP: dp[i] = dp[i & (i-1)] + 1, reusing a smaller already-computed answer
Sort the numbers and binary search for each one's bit count
XOR all the numbers together and read off the result
Use two pointers scanning across the range 0..n
items.answer: Precompute with a bitwise DP: dp[i] = dp[i & (i-1)] + 1, reusing a smaller already-computed answer
items.explanation_md: Each i's bit count differs from i & (i-1)'s by exactly one bit, so a DP table built from smaller indices answers the whole range in O(n), no per-number bit loop needed.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of building the full 0..n bit-count table with the `dp[i] = dp[i & (i-1)] + 1` recurrence?
items.explanation_md: Each table entry does O(1) work (one AND, one lookup, one increment) reusing a smaller already-computed answer, so filling all n+1 entries is O(n) time and O(n) space for the table.
